title: Considerations for Corneal Surgery With Patients in the 10th Decade of Life.
abstract: The purpose of this study was to report the indications, ocular and systemic comorbidities, and surgical outcomes of corneal transplantation in patients older than 90 years. A retrospective review was conducted to identify individuals 90 years and older who underwent corneal transplantation surgery at the Bascom Palmer Eye Institute between January 2013 and October 2020. Outcomes included best-corrected visual acuity and graft survival over time. Paired t tests were used to compare visual acuity preoperatively versus postoperatively. Graft survival was evaluated with Kaplan-Meier curves. Fifty-eight eyes of 52 consecutive individuals were included. The mean age of individuals was 92 ± 2 years; 26.9% were male; and 48.1% self-identified as non-Hispanic White and 38.5% as Hispanic. Postoperative follow-up was 14.7 ± 12.1 months. Of the 58 eyes, 44.8% (26/58) underwent penetrating keratoplasty, 46.6% (27/58) Descemet stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty, and 6.9% (4/58) keratoprosthesis. All surgeries were performed under monitored local anesthesia, without major complications. Surgical indications included pseudophakic bullous keratopathy (36.2%), glaucoma-associated corneal decompensation (27.6%), Fuchs endothelial dystrophy (25.9%), and perforated corneal ulceration (19.0%). The best-corrected visual acuity improved by 0.32 (95% confidence interval 0.14-0.50; P < 0.01) as early as 1 month postoperatively, and vision gains were sustained for at least 12 months. Graft survival probability at 12 months was 88%. Corneal transplantation is a safe and successful procedure in restoring the visual acuity for patients older than 90 years after careful preoperative evaluation. Further research is needed to evaluate the impact of corneal transplantation on quality of life in patients in the 10th decade of life.
full_text:
Considerations for Corneal Surgery with Patients in the Tenth Decade of Life

Purpose:

To report the indications, ocular and systemic comorbidities, and surgical outcomes of cornea transplantation in patients over 90 years of age.

Methods:

A retrospective review was conducted to identify individuals 90 years and older who underwent corneal transplantation surgery at the Bascom Palmer Eye Institute between January 2013 and October 2020. Outcomes included best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) and graft survival over time. Paired t-tests were utilized to compare visual acuity pre- vs post-operatively. Graft survival was evaluated with Kaplan-Meier curves.

Results:

58 eyes of 52 consecutive individuals were included. The mean age was 92±2 years; 26.9% were male; 48.1% self-identified as non-Hispanic White and 38.5% as Hispanic. Post-operative follow-up was 14.7±12.1 months. 44.8% (26/58) underwent penetrating keratoplasty, 46.6% (27/58) Descemet-stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty and 6.9% (4/58) keratoprosthesis. All surgeries were done under monitored local anesthesia, without major complications. Surgical indications included pseudophakic bullous keratopathy (36.2%), glaucoma-associated corneal decompensation (27.6%), Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy (25.9%), and perforated corneal ulceration (19.0%). BCVA improved by 0.32 (95% CI 0.14–0.50; p<0.01) as early as one month post-operatively, and vision gains were sustained for at least 12 months. Graft survival probability at 12 months was 88%.

Conclusions:

Corneal transplantation is a safe and successful procedure in restoring visual acuity for patients over 90 years-old after careful preoperative evaluation. Further research is needed to evaluate the impact of corneal transplantation on quality of life in patients in the 10th decade of life.

BACKGROUND

Corneal blindness is the fifth most prevalent cause of blindness worldwide. Corneal transplantation can be successfully and safely performed in most of these patients to recover vision, and it is the most common type of organ transplant performed worldwide. Historically, full-thickness penetrating keratoplasty (PKP) has been the most common type of corneal transplant performed.  However, in the last decade there has been an increase of other techniques including endothelial and anterior lamellar transplantation procedures, such as Descemet membrane automated endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK), Descemet stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK), deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK), as well as the use of keratoprosthesis (K-Pro) in patients that are not candidates for any other form of corneal transplantation.

Life expectancy has increased in the last decade, and as a result there has been a rise in the elderly population. Based on the 2012 National Projection of the United States (US), it is estimated that in 2050, those aged 85 and over will account for 4.5 percent of the US population. It is expected than the number of individuals with visual impairment will more than double by 2050, with those aged 80 and above at a higher risk. Visual impairment in the elderly population is associated with increased fall risk, 1-year mortality and functional decline. While the etiology of the decreased vision is variable and different factors (visual acuity, contrast and glare sensitivity, stereoacuity, and visual fields) can play a role in the self-reported visual disability, loss of corneal transparency may contribute, and cornea transplantation is one of the procedures that may be required in the elderly population to increase vision and quality of life.

Some of the main challenges in the surgical management of this population are the numerous medical comorbidities leading to potential complications both intra- and post- operatively, and the inability to remain in appropriate positioning during and/or after the procedure. There has been discordance on whether general anesthesia increases long-term post-operative cognitive dysfunction when compared to regional anesthesia, but it is thought that the latter is associated with lower incidence of cognitive impairment in the early postoperative stages. Typically, monitored regional anesthesia is preferred for ophthalmic procedures in elderly populations because it allows for prompt interventions if needed, while maintaining appropriate pain control.

Despite the increasing aging population, there is limited evidence of the indications and visual outcomes of cornea transplantation in elderly individuals, particularly among those over the age of 90 years old. Parker et al evaluated DMEK surgery in patients over age 90, with good surgical and visual outcomes. However, to our knowledge there are no studies evaluating other corneal surgery modalities in this age group. To fill this gap, the purpose of this study was to evaluate the indications, outcomes and safety of corneal transplantation in patients at 90 years of age and above. This study assessed the medical comorbidities of this population as well as the complications to anesthesia needed for these procedures, the ocular comorbidities, best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) before and after cornea surgery, and graft survival over time. This study may serve as guidance for the ophthalmic and anesthetic considerations related to corneal surgery in this complex group of patients for more informed counseling and consent with patients, family members, guardians, and care providers.

METHODS:

Study population

Patients over the age of 90 years old who underwent corneal transplantation at the Bascom Palmer Eye Institute in Miami, FL between January 2013 and October 2020 were included in this study. Individuals were identified based on the specimens list of the Florida Lions Eye Bank. A retrospective chart review was conducted and demographics, ocular and systemic comorbidities, surgical history, visual acuities, and graft status over time were recorded. If a patient had more than one corneal transplant surgery in the same eye at the age of 90 years or older, only the last surgery was considered for analysis. There were 9 individuals (5 males and 4 females) that had at least two corneal transplants in the same eye after the age of 90 years old, for a total of 20 transplants (7 DSAEK, 4 K-pro, 1 corneal patch graft, 8 PKP). A total of 70 eyes of 63 individuals over the age of 90 years old that underwent cornea transplantation were identified for this study. Individuals were grouped based on their follow-up time after surgery, using ≥3 months follow up as the cutoff point. Individuals with less than 3 months follow up (n = 11) were excluded from further analyses. A total of 58 eyes of 52 patients were then grouped based on improvement in BCVA during the third month after surgery to evaluate ocular comorbidities, indications for surgery, type of transplant received, and additional interventions required. Improvement in BCVA was defined as having a lower numerical logMAR value during the third post-operative month compared to pre-operatively.

Surgical procedures

All donor corneal tissue was obtained from the Florida Lions Eye Bank. Penetrating Keratoplasty (PKP), Descemet Stripping Automated Endothelial Keratoplasty (DSAEK), and Boston type I K-Pro (Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary, Boston, MA, USA) were performed with standard surgical technique as previously described by others. All transplants were performed under monitored anesthesia care and peribulbar nerve block or retrobulbar nerve block. The medications used for intravenous sedation were a combination of midazolam and fentanyl or remifentanil. Major regional anesthesia-related complications were evaluated, and were defined as globe rupture, retrobulbar hemorrhage, nerve injury, seizures, cardiac arrest, respiratory failure, or death.

PKP donor grafts were prepared with a disposable trephine of 7–9 mm for a graft-host oversized by 0.25 mm. After marking the host cornea and creating a temporal paracentesis, viscoelastic was injected. The host cornea was then trephined to near full-thickness with a Hessburg-Barron suction trephine (Barron Precision Instruments, Grand Blanc, MI, USA) and then excised with curved corneal scissors. The donor cornea was then trephined and transferred into position on top of the host bed. Then, the donor cornea was secured in place with sixteen interrupted 10–0 nylon sutures.

In the case of DSAEK surgeries, a 7–9 mm manual trephine was used to mark the optical zone of the cornea. Two paracentesis sites were created, and viscoelastic solution injected. A temporal tri-planar clear corneal incision was created with a keratome blade. The host Descemet’s membrane and endothelium were scored in a circular fashion just outside of the optical zone mark with a reverse Sinskey hook (Bausch & Lomb, San Dimas, CA) and stripped with the use of a Descemet’s stripper (Katena, Denville, NJ). Viscoelastic was removed with irrigation and aspiration. Grafts were precut to a thickness of 80–90μm by FLEB. The graft was trephined to 7–9 mm, separated from the donor stroma, and placed in the anterior chamber and unfolded with balanced salt solution. Then the anterior chamber was completely filled with air, and the patient was maintained in face-up position for at least 5 minutes. A reverse Sinskey hook was then used to position the graft centrally. Lastly, an air fluid exchange was performed, and a mobile air bubble of similar size to the corneal button was left in the anterior chamber. Cycloplegic drops were then applied to the eye. Patients were instructed to remain in the supine face-up position for at least 1 hour for proper graft attachment.

Boston Type I keratoprosthesis (K-pro) were implanted in individuals of advanced age ineligible for other corneal transplantation modalities (due to conditions such as, multiple graft failures or hypotonia) to expedite visual recovery by eliminating the need for early suture removal for the management of astigmatism. For these procedures, Snap-On aphakic Boston K-pro type 1 implants (Massachusetts Eye and Ear Infirmary, Boston, MA, USA) were used. Patients were either previously aphakic or were rendered so during the procedure. Pars-Plana Vitrectomies were performed on all K-pro surgeries. A donor button measuring 7–9 mm with 3 mm central hole was prepared with a trephine. Then, the donor cornea button was placed over the stem of the front plate, and the back plate was screwed into place along a titanium lockring. The host cornea was prepared in a similar fashion as a PKP, and the donor graft with the K-Pro in place was secured with sixteen interrupted 10–0 nylon sutures.

Depending on the type of transplant, concomitant procedures (e.g., removal or exchange of Intraocular Lens [IOL], vitrectomy, tarsorrhaphy) were performed at the discretion of the surgeon based on patient clinical presentation. Antibiotic and corticosteroid drops were applied to all surgical eyes and tapered, as appropriate. Topical corticosteroid therapy was prescribed for long-term use to prevent graft failure or rejection.

Post-operative evaluation

Patients were evaluated after the surgery on a routine basis, and standardized physical exam was performed that included slit-lamp biomicroscopic examination; visual acuity measured with a Snellen chart; and intraocular pressure (IOP) measured with applanation tonometry, tonopen (Medtronic, Doral, FL, USA) or by optic nerve imaging and visual field evaluation twice a year in the case of keratoprosthesis. The main outcomes evaluated were the BCVA and graft survival over time. BCVA measurements were recorded in Snellen chart at the pre-operative time as well as during the follow-up appointments after the procedure. BCVA values were converted to logarithm of minimal angle resolution (LogMAR) for statistical analysis. Visual acuity equivalents for count fingers, hand motion, light perception and no light perception were assigned logMAR values of 1.85, 2.30, 2.70 and 3.00 respectively, as previously described

Graft status was assessed based on clinical examination findings. The graft survival was calculated from the date of the surgery to the last date of follow-up, or to the date of failure or rejection of graft. High-dose topical corticosteroid therapy was promptly administered upon any clinical suspicion of graft failure or rejection. Graft failure was defined as irreversible opacification and edema persistent after at least one month of high-dose topical corticosteroid therapy; graft rejection was defined as the development of an endothelial rejection line or signs of inflammation, such as keratic precipitates, increase in stromal or aqueous cells irrespective of clinically significant changes in stromal thickness or clarity. Patients were grouped by type of transplant (PKP, DSAEK or K-Pro); given that only one corneal patch was performed, this was excluded from the curve. Subsequently, PKPs were sub-divided based on optical or therapeutic surgery indications and their respective graft survivals over time were evaluated. Therapeutic indications included those transplants performed due to an active microbial infection at risk of perforation or a cornea with an active perforation; optical indications included all other surgical indications. Kaplan-Meier curves were constructed to represent the survival of the grafts over time.

Statistical analysis

Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS statistical package version 26.0 (IBM Corp, Armonk, NY). Data were represented by frequencies, averages, and standard deviation as applicable. Independent samples t-tests and Fisher’s exact tests were used as appropriate. Paired t-tests were used to compare BCVA and IOP at different selected timepoints against pre-operative values. For purpose of the statistical analysis of BCVA and IOP, cases were removed list-wise and only those with up to 12 months follow-up were evaluated, since the number of patients was reduced drastically for the timepoints after that. In the case of IOP measurements, only numerical values were included. To evaluate the survival of the grafts, Kaplan-Meier survival analyses were performed, and log-rank tests were used to estimate the probabilities of graft survival over time between groups. All tests performed were two-tailed and significance was considered at a p-value<0.05.

Ethical statement

This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) at the University of Miami Miller School of Medicine (IRB#20201232) and was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and the United States Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act.

RESULTS:

Study population:

A total of 70 eyes of 63 consecutive cases of individuals over the age of 90 years old that underwent cornea transplantation were identified for this study. A total of 11 individuals were excluded from further analysis because they had a shorter than 3 months follow up time after corneal surgery. Among all individuals, 2 eyes had to be enucleated within the first month after surgery due to persistent pain with no visual improvement; 4 individuals were lost to follow up because they moved to a different country; and 4 individuals died within the first post-operative year. There were no differences in demographics, ocular, and systemic comorbidities between included and excluded patients.

In this study, 58 eyes of 52 individuals with at least three months post-operative follow up were included, their demographics and systemic comorbidities are presented in Table 1. Their mean age was 92.2±2.0 years at the time of surgery; 26.9% of them were males; 48.1% self- identified as non-Hispanic White, 5.8% as non-Hispanic Black or African American and 38.4% as Hispanic. The average post-operative follow-up time was 14.7 ± 12.1 months [range 3–61 months]. A total of 42 patients were followed up for 6 months; 34 for 12 months; 20 for 18 months; 11 for 24 months; and 4 for 36 months or more. The most common systemic comorbidities were hypertension (80.8%), arthritis (61.5%), and hyperlipidemia (40.4%).

Pre-existing ocular comorbidities:

The preoperative ocular comorbidities and findings of patients over the age of 90 years old at the time of corneal transplantation, grouped by improvement of BCVA 3 months after surgery are presented in table 2. Glaucoma was the most common ocular comorbidity in both groups with 60% of individuals in those with BCVA improvement at three months and 72.2% among those without BCVA improvement at three months (p-0.56). Patients with BCVA improvement 3 months after undergoing corneal transplantation were significantly less likely to have pseudoexfoliation glaucoma (PXFG) compared to patients that did not have BCVA improvement at 3 months after surgery (7.5% vs 33.3%, p-0.02).

Surgical data:

None of the individuals had major complications related to anesthesia, but one patient had intraoperative asymptomatic bradycardia. Moreover, three surgeries had to be rescheduled at least one week due to acute-on chronic kidney disease, severe hypertension, or epigastric pain before the surgery. One patient was unable to stay flat, and the surgery was performed at a semi-recumbent position with no complications.

The types of transplants performed, indications for transplantation and additional interventions required, grouped by improvement of BCVA in the third month follow up after surgery are presented in table 3. Most patients that had an improvement of BCVA in 3 months after surgery had a DSAEK (50%) and the most common indication for surgery was pseudophakic bullous keratopathy (PBK; 45%). In the patients that did not have BCVA improvement in 3 months, PKP was the most common type of surgery (50%) and the most common indication for surgery was Fuchs endothelial dystrophy (38.9%). Additional surgical interventions were required in 57.5% of patients with BCVA improvement and in 72.2% of patients without.

Surgical Complications:

The surgical complications of patients over the age of 90 that underwent corneal transplantation, grouped by type of graft received are presented in table 4. A total of 15.8% of all grafts failed or were rejected; this represents 3.7% of all DSAEKs performed, 26.9% of PKPs and 100% of corneal patch grafts. Among patients that had DSAEK, there was one case (3.7%) of pupillary block requiring peripheral iridotomy; and five cases (18.5%) of partial graft detachment, with four out of them (80%) recovering vision after repositioning with re-bubbling, performed at either the slit-lamp (1 case), minor procedure room (1 case), or operating room (2 cases). Among those that had a PKP, complications included one patient (3.8%) with an intraoperative non-expulsive suprachoroidal hemorrhage, two with high astigmatism (7.6%), one with hypotony (3.8%) and two with persistently high IOP (7.6%). Among those that had a K-pro one patient (25%) developed a retro-lenticular membrane. Post-operative wound leakage on the first post-operative day occurred in 3.8% of PKPs, 3.7% of DSAEKs and 25% of K-Pros, and resolved in all cases with medical management. There were no cases of post-operative ocular trauma.

Post-operative data:

Visual Acuity and intraocular pressure

The BCVA and IOP progression of patients of patients over the age of 90 years old that underwent corneal transplantation is presented in figure 1; mean parameters are shown on the left side and frequencies on the right side by post-operative month. Before the surgery, 54.4% of patients had a vision of 2.30 or worse which was reduced to 21.1% in the third month and to 15.4% in the twelfth month after surgery. In the first month after surgery BCVA improved 0.32 (95% CI 0.14–0.50; p<0.01). In the sixth month, improvement of BCVA was 0.63 (95% CI 0.42–0.84; p<0.001) and 12 months after surgery BCVA improved 0.43 (95% CI 0.18–0.68; p<0.01) (Figure 1A). There was no difference in post-operative BCVA progression over time by transplant type (data not shown).

In terms of IOP, 37% of patients had a measurement between 11–15mmHg before the surgery, 31.3% had an IOP of 16–20 mmHg in month 3 after the surgery and 33.3% had 16–20mmHg in month 12. There was a significant increase in the IOP of 3.2mmHg (95% CI 0.5–5.8; p-0.02) in the third month after surgery compared to the pre-operative period (Figure 1B)

Graft survival

Kaplan-Meier curves were conducted to represent the corneal graft probability of survival over time (Figure 2). At 12 months, probability of survival was 88% for all grafts and 66% at 24 months (Figure 2A). When stratifying the corneal grafts based on type of transplant (Figure 2B), it was found that at 12 months following surgery, the probability of survival was 85% for full-thickness grafts, 94% for partial grafts and 100% for K-pro; at 24 months the probability of survival was 47% for PKPs, 94% for DSAEKs and 100% for K-Pros (p<0.01). We further sup-grouped full-thickness transplants by optical or therapeutic indications (Figure 2C). It was found that at 12 and 18 months the probability of survival for optical PKPs was 92% and 83% respectively compared to 66% and 33% for therapeutic transplants (p-0.03).

DISCUSSION

This study provides an evaluation of the indications and outcomes of corneal transplant in patients over the age of 90 years old. The most common indication for transplant in our population was PBK, which is in accordance to previously reported data. In our study, corneal decompensation secondary to glaucoma was found to be a slightly more common indication than Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy, in contrast with other studies. Prevalence of glaucoma was considerably higher (63.8%) than that reported among patients over age 80 years old (8.3%) Moreover, a considerable proportion of our patients with glaucoma had previous glaucoma surgery and a previous corneal transplant. While a previous transplant has been associated with decreased improvement in visual acuity,  and decreased survival probability with subsequent transplants, there is contrasting evidence regarding glaucoma surgery effect on graft survival. Despite the prevalence of glaucoma in our population, the IOP remained relatively unchanged throughout the post-operative period, with exception of the third month after surgery. Given the relative recovery of IOP afterwards, this increase could have been secondary to the corticosteroid therapy.

Despite their ocular comorbidities, there was a significant improvement of at least 3 lines in the BCVA within the first month post-operatively, which was relatively sustained for at least 12 months after surgery. While the exact impact of improving visual acuity after corneal transplantation in this population has not been reported, visual impairment has been previously associated with increased fall risk, mortality rate and functional decline among elderly individuals. Furthermore, increasing age has been associated with a higher likelihood of self-reported quality of life improvement after corneal transplantations among individuals 65 years or older. Patients that had an improvement in visual acuity three months after surgery were more likely to have PBK and less likely to have PXFG than those that did not have BCVA improvement in three months. The prevalence of PXFG in our population was found to be 15.5%, similar to prior studies (5%−27%). Given that PXFG tends to have a more aggressive clinical course than other causes of open angle glaucoma, it is possible that the lack of visual acuity improvement in individuals with PXFG was due to decompensation of endothelial cells secondary to increased intraocular pressures. Moreover, some studies have suggested that the accumulation of pseudoexfoliation material can further decompensate endothelial cells.

At 12 months follow up, the graft survival was estimated to be 88%, in accordance to previously reported graft survival in elderly patients. When classifying the graft survival by type of transplant, it was noticed that DSAEK (94%) and K-pro (100%) had a significantly higher probability of survival than full-thickness transplants (85%) at 12 months after surgery. Previous studies have shown a graft survival rate of 81%−100% for DSAEKs, and 89%−99% for K-pro at 12 months. It is worth noting that roughly a third of individuals in our study received therapeutic transplants due to an impending perforated ulcer, and those grafts tend to have lower survival rates due to the increased ocular inflammation at the time of surgery. In our study, therapeutic grafts had a survival probability of 66% in 12 months, compared to 92% for optical full-thickness grafts during the same period. Prior studies have shown a survival rate of 47%−78% for therapeutic transplants depending on their exact etiology, time of onset, and size of graft needed, compared to 87%−95% for optical full-thickness transplants. While none of the individuals in our cohort had a traumatic post-operative graft rupture, the fall risk in the elderly population must be considered given the propensity of ocular injury compromising corneal graft integrity, and appropriate safety glass protection should be emphasized.

A total of 18.5% of DSAEK cases in our population developed a partial graft detachment, in accordance to previously reported rates of 4.9%−23% Given the comorbidities in our population, postoperative supine face-up positioning could have been difficult to maintain. However, while postoperative supine position is often preferred for endothelial cell grafts, there has been studies showing there is no difference in graft detachment rates after DSAEK when comparing individuals in supine or sitting post-operative positioning, as long as the anterior chamber is completely filled with air. In our study, 80% of endothelial graft detachment cases were successfully reattached with improvement of visual acuity through re-bubbling, in accordance to a reported success rate of 74–76% for this procedure. Only one patient that underwent DSAEK (3.7%) developed a pupillary block requiring peripheral iridotomy treatment; this is similar to previously reported incidence of 2.0%−6.9%. Among patients that received a K-pro, one patient (25%) developed a retro-lenticular membrane, which is lower compared to previously reported rates of 33%−45.5%.

In our study, one individual with a PKP developed a non-expulsive suprachoroidal hemorrhage, that while rare, it has been previously described after corneal transplant with an estimated occurrence of 0.73%. This devastating complication has been associated with risk factors such as advanced age, hypertension and glaucoma. A significant portion of elderly patients are prescribed antithrombotic agents for prevention and management of their systemic comorbidities. The decision to maintain or discontinue these medications prior to corneal surgery is predicated on the balance between potential vision-threatening hemorrhagic complications versus life-threatening thromboembolic events, including stroke, myocardial infarction, pulmonary embolism, or deep venous thrombosis. Bleeding complications are an inherent risk of surgery, but it remains unclear if preoperative antithrombotics increase the incidence of significant hemorrhage. There have been studies evaluating the continued preoperative use of antithrombotic agents, in which they were not associated with an increased risk of hyphema during operative iridotomy in DMEK. However, to our knowledge there are no specific studies on perioperative antithrombotics in full thickness corneal transplant. Hemorrhage typically has minimal visual consequence, while systemic sequelae from withdrawal of antithrombotics may have substantive impact on morbidity and mortality. Therefore, the significant risks of interrupting a patient’s antithrombotic regimen must be carefully measured against the low but real risk of surgical bleeding.

Nonagenarian corneal surgery candidates typically have multiple comorbidities which may require delay of surgery to allow for optimization of physical status, such as recent myocardial infarction, decompensated cardiac failure, dysrhythmias that compromise hemodynamics (including ventricular tachycardia, and atrial fibrillation with rapid heart rate), recent stroke, altered mental status, pulmonary embolism, symptomatic pneumonia and more.  In our study population, three surgeries had to be delayed due to various systemic comorbidities. However, after appropriate medical clearance and clinical optimization, the surgeries occurred without major complications associated with anesthesia, and were well tolerated by the patients overall. A multidisciplinary approach for the medical evaluation and management prior to surgery, including consultation with the patient’s medical team, and evaluation by the treating anesthesiologist is paramount to ensure patient safety and good surgical outcomes.

Due to the comorbidities in this age group, general anesthesia tends to be avoided in outpatient centers, where most ophthalmic surgeries are performed, as they lack clinical support to solve complications associated to this type of anesthesia. Although general anesthesia could be suitable for long and difficult procedures, there has been an increase in the use of monitored regional anesthesia in combination with mild sedation, as an alternative in ophthalmic surgery. In our study, one patient developed asymptomatic bradycardia intraoperatively, which was appropriately managed. Given the number of cofounders, it is difficult to pinpoint the exact trigger for bradycardia in this individual, but it could have been caused by the oculocardiac reflex. While it has a rare incidence it is a well-documented cause of asymptomatic bradycardia in ophthalmic surgery, thought to be induced by mechanical manipulation of the eye, and it can be augmented by the use of fast-acting opioids such as fentanyl. If not treated promptly, it can lead to complications such as cardiac arrest. A well-planned surgical procedure and careful considerations with anesthesia can avoid these complications. It is imperative for ophthalmic anesthesia providers to understand the risks and benefits of the type of anesthesia, according to the planned ocular surgery that will be performed.

The increasing aging population and subsequent potential for increased demand for corneal transplantation surgery could bring some challenges in the future of corneal tissue banking, given increased logistical and ethical considerations, such as the need for transnational exporting and fair tissue allocation. Currently, it is estimated that 12.7 million people are waiting for corneal transplant, with the majority of them living in locations with limited access to suitable donor corneal tissue. Scoring systems have been proposed for the prioritization of optical transplants based on parameters, such as visual potential, discomfort level, infection risk, waiting time, and estimated success rate.  However, there is no widespread system in place and each nation designates its own allocation system. Novel strategies may be needed to reduce the demand of donor tissue, such as splitting and using a single donor cornea for multiple partial graft recipients. Promising procedures that could reduce the need for donor corneal tissue are currently being evaluated and include the use of stem cell treatments for endothelial cell replacement, and the use of biosynthetic corneal tissue.

There are some limitations associated with the study that should be elucidated for the proper contextual interpretation of the conclusions presented. First, the number of patients included in this study is relatively small, and only those individuals that underwent a corneal transplant were included. There is no data regarding the number or conditions of patients that were seen at the tertiary level hospital at the age of 90 years and above and did not have a surgical procedure done. It is difficult to conclude with certainty which individuals would benefit from undergoing surgery. Thus, surgical, and clinical management should be individualized to each patient. Second, our population consisted mostly of Caucasian elderly females seeking care at a tertiary eye-care hospital and the results may not be generalized to other populations of individuals over the age of 90 years old. Third, only the last surgery performed was included for eyes that had multiple corneal transplants at 90 years or older. Most of the individuals in the study were referred by outside ophthalmologists and/or corneal specialists and thus we have limited data regarding prior corneal transplants. We felt including the last graft performed would give clinicians a more accurate representation of nonagenarian patients coming for surgical evaluation and better represent their short-term outcomes. This is relevant in this population given the limited life expectancy and degree of pre-operative visual impairment. Lastly, some individuals were excluded based on their follow-up time after surgery being less than three months. However, given that excluded patients had similar demographics and systemic comorbidities as those included in further analyses, selection bias is unlikely. Moreover, 35% (18/52) of individuals were lost to follow up by the twelfth month post-operatively, of which 44% (8/18) were attributed to death or relocation to a different region. It is possible that the remaining individuals were lost to follow up due to unconfirmed death, or logistical and/or physical difficulty in returning for evaluation at our institution and were followed up by their local ophthalmologists or corneal specialists.

Despite the limitations, the data presented are important as it suggest that corneal transplantation surgery in patients over the age of 90 years old is a safe procedure with minimal complications to regional anesthesia, and successful with the potential of significantly improving visual acuity. Special considerations must be taken in the surgical and clinical management of these patients, including close collaboration between patient physicians, anesthesiologists, and ophthalmic surgeons. Further studies are needed to evaluate the direct impact on the quality of life and functional status in this complex population after undergoing corneal transplantation during the 10th decade of life.

Declarations of Interest: None
